rejected.headline: India's Cockroach Janta Party Protesters Return After Police Crackdown
reasons: The draft states 'Thousands of protesters gathered again in Delhi on July 22, one day after police used batons and tear gas to disperse a demonstration' and later says 'On July 21, tens of thousands of young men and women defied a police ban to march on parliament.' The brief's core_claim and data_points describe the July 21 crackdown and July 22 return, but the draft does not clearly distinguish that the July 21 event was a single march/attempt that day, not a multi-day sustained presence before the crackdown. The phrasing 'gathered again' implies they were there before July 21, but per the brief, the protest camp had been at Jantar Mantar 'for the past month' (data_point 9), the crackdown occurred on July 21, and the return was July 22. The draft's narrative structure risks conflating the prior month-long camp with a July 21 daytime march. The opening should make clear the temporal sequence: month-long camp → July 21 march attempt → police crackdown → July 22 return to protest site.

The draft's bottom_line states: 'the police crackdown on unarmed protesters—including women—and the government's initial dismissal signal a deepening domestic political crisis.' The characterization of protesters as 'unarmed' is not present in the brief's data_points. The brief documents that police said 118 of their personnel were injured and protesters displayed 'unruly, aggressive and violent behaviour,' and the bear_case includes that police injury claims 'suggest protesters displayed resistance that created risk to law enforcement.' The draft's unqualified 'unarmed' descriptor is an inference not supported by the sourced record and reads as editorial framing of the police justification (which is based on resistance/violence claims, not weapons). This violates the rule against smuggling unsourced characterizations into the draft.

The draft conflates these—attributing the 'provisionally forecast' language to the government when the brief attributes it to the OBR. This is an accuracy error: the source of the forecast matters.

The brief does not provide the names of the other three, so the draft cannot name them either—but as written, it implies four senior leaders were identified when only one is named, which is misleading.

Yet male athletes are stealing medals, invading locker rooms, and putting girls in the hospital, all in the name of gender ideology. Our NDAA amendment would end this nonsense.' In a prepared statement, according to Fox News, Mace said...' The attribution is technically present but the framing ('Mace said... stating that... In a prepared statement, according to Fox News, Mace said') is awkwardly layered and could be read as padding the Mace voice without additional fact-checking.

rejected.headline: Largest cyclosporiasis outbreak on record expands to nine states with 11,500+ cases
reasons: The draft states 'more than 11,500 people suspected to have been sickened this summer' but the brief's open_questions flag a discrepancy: 'The headline states "CDC investigates 7,400 cases" but the snippet and article text reference "more than 11,500 suspected cases"—which figure is current and what accounts for the discrepancy?' The writer has adopted one figure (11,500) without acknowledging this unresolved conflict in the source material. The brief marks the source as thin and only 2,019 characters; the writer has no basis to choose between competing figures from Guardian reporting.

The draft states the outbreak 'has been linked to contaminated iceberg lettuce' as fact, but the brief's open_questions include 'What is the confirmed source of the contamination (the snippet mentions "iceberg lettuce" but this is not verified in the provided source text)?' The iceberg lettuce link is not confirmed in the brief's data_points—only mentioned as unverified in an open question. Presenting it as settled fact violates the accuracy standard.

rejected.headline: Romania shoots down unidentified drone near Black Sea in first NATO airspace incident
reasons: The draft states 'This is the first time Romania as a NATO member state has shot down a drone in its own airspace' but the brief data_point says 'This marks the first time the NATO state has shot down a drone in its own airspace.' The draft adds 'as a NATO member state'—a qualifier not in the brief. The brief does not claim this is NATO's first such action, only Romania's. The draft's next sentence states 'marking the first time Romania as a NATO member has shot down a drone in its own airspace, according to The Hill' and later the bottom_line says 'This is the first air defense action of its kind by a NATO member in its own airspace'—conflating Romania's first action with NATO's first action. These are distinct claims; the draft smuggled the NATO-wide claim without source support in the brief.

rejected.headline: US and Iran trade strikes as June ceasefire collapses into military escalation
reasons: The draft states 'Iran carried out drone strikes on US military facilities across the Middle East on the 13th consecutive night of American attacks' but the brief data_point says 'Iran says it carried out drone strikes' and 'Iran claims it launched attacks'—the draft presents Iran's claims as established fact without the qualifier that these are Iranian assertions. The confidence label is 'reported' but the prose reads as verified.

The draft includes two data_points not traced to the brief: (1) 'Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu is scheduled to meet with Trump at the White House on Tuesday' (data_point 21) and (2) 'Netanyahu is scheduled to attend a service for the late Senator Lindsey Graham. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky is expected to meet with Trump the same day' (data_point 22) appear in the brief but do not appear in the draft body. While these are in the brief, they are unrelated to the core story of US-Iran escalation and their absence from the draft is not a violation, but their presence in the brief without use raises a question of editorial judgment rather than accuracy violation. However, the main accuracy issue stands: the draft treats reported claims as fact.

rejected.headline: Iran Conflict and Trump Tariffs Create Dual Economic Pressure on U.S. Growth
reasons: The draft states 'Renewed fighting with Iran is sending oil and gas prices soaring' and 'the Trump administration has announced new global tariffs,' but neither claim specifies that these are reported by the New York Times in the manner required by the brief's data_points. The draft attributes the conflict claim only in a parenthetical attribution ('According to reporting by the New York Times') but then proceeds to discuss the tariffs and dual shock without attributing them to the same source.

The draft asserts 'New tariffs raise the cost of imported goods, from appliances to clothing to automobiles' as a mechanistic fact without sourcing. This claim goes beyond what the brief's data_point states ('could push prices higher'), moving from reported possibility to presented certainty.

The final paragraph of the body ('The brief does not specify...') is a meta-commentary that violates the hard rule: no fact exists outside the brief. This sentence itself is not a fact from the brief and constitutes the writer commenting on the research rather than reporting from it.

rejected.headline: Judge Orders Pentagon to Explain Testosterone Policy Disparity Between Troops
reasons: The draft states 'Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth has championed a plan to screen older soldiers for testosterone deficiencies and offer them hormone treatment,' but the brief's data_point describes the policy as 'controversial.' The draft omits the confidence label and presents the policy as established fact rather than as reported/controversial.

The final paragraph of the body ('The brief does not indicate...') is a meta-commentary that violates the hard rule. This sentence is not a fact from the brief and constitutes writer commentary on the research rather than reporting.

The draft characterizes the policy's constitutional status by stating it 'raises constitutional equal protection questions' without attributing this legal analysis to the court order or the Guardian reporting. The brief provides no data_point supporting an independent assertion of what constitutional question is at stake—only that the judge ordered an explanation of the differential treatment.

rejected.headline: Russian ballistic missiles kill 10 at Ukrainian drone exhibition near Kyiv
reasons: The draft states 'At least 15 people were killed across Ukraine in Russian attacks on July 24' but the brief's first data point says 'At least 15 people killed in Russian attacks on Ukraine overall on July 24, 2026, including 10 dead in ballistic missile strikes on a drone exhibition near Kyiv.' The draft presents this as a standalone figure at the end without clarity that the 10 at the exhibition are part of the 15 total, creating potential confusion about the actual death count.

The draft includes a paragraph about Zelensky traveling to the US, meeting Trump on Tuesday, and attending Senator Lindsey Graham's funeral. The brief data point says Trump ally Laura Loomer 'interviewed Zelensky, who stated he intends to go to the funeral of US Senator Lindsey Graham on the same day as his planned meeting with Trump.' The draft omits the crucial detail that Laura Loomer was in Kyiv and conducted this interview, and presents Zelensky's statements about his schedule without that context, which changes the sourcing attribution.

rejected.headline: Six killed in West Bank shooting; accounts conflict on who initiated violence
reasons: The brief contains only one data point confirming the death toll (four Palestinians and two Israelis). All other claims in the draft—that accounts conflict on who initiated violence, that the specific sequence of events remains undetailed, that Israel deployed troops as a response, that deployment scope/positioning is unspecified—are not present in the brief and therefore constitute smuggled facts written outside the research.

The draft's framing that 'Official accounts from Israeli and Palestinian authorities conflict' is presented as fact, but the brief contains no data point establishing what these conflicting accounts are or that they even exist in the source material. This is a major factual claim unsupported by the research.

rejected.headline: Cyclosporiasis outbreak expands to nine states with 11,500 suspected cases
reasons: The draft states 'This is the largest cyclosporiasis outbreak on record in the United States' as a definitive fact, but the brief data_point reads 'This is the largest cyclosporiasis outbreak on record' without the 'in the United States' qualifier. The draft has added geographic specificity not present in the sourced claim.

The draft claims 'Cyclosporiasis, a parasitic infection, can cause explosive diarrhea and other gastrointestinal symptoms, per The Guardian.' The brief only confirms 'Cyclosporiasis can cause explosive diarrhea' as the sourced claim. The addition of 'parasitic infection' as a defining characteristic and 'other gastrointestinal symptoms' beyond explosive diarrhea extends beyond what the brief supports and should be verified against the source.

rejected.headline: Teen pleads guilty to all 55 counts in 2024 Apalachee High School shooting that killed four
reasons: The draft states 'The shooting, which occurred nearly two years ago, killed two students and two teachers' but the brief's timestamp is 2026-07-24 and refers to a '2024 shooting,' which would be roughly 1.5-2 years prior depending on the month in 2024. The phrasing 'nearly two years ago' is imprecise given the brief's own language ('2024') and creates factual ambiguity about timing that the brief does not resolve. The brief explicitly notes 'What is the exact date of the shooting' as an open question, meaning the writer cannot assert temporal claims beyond what the source provides.

rejected.headline: UNABLE TO DRAFT: Source material inaccessible
reasons: The draft is presented as an inability-to-draft notice rather than a news story. The brief confirms the source material is inaccessible (source_chars: 312, only metadata available). The draft correctly identifies this as a blocker, but it should not be submitted as a 'draft' for approval—it is a routing instruction to research. This violates the output contract: the desk does not approve meta-commentary about missing sources as finished editorial product.

rejected.headline: UNABLE TO DRAFT: Source material contains no data points
reasons: The draft is presented as an inability-to-draft notice rather than a news story. The brief confirms data_points is empty and source_chars is 0—no substantive content exists. Like c76, this is a routing instruction to research, not a finished draft. It should not be submitted for approval as editorial product.

rejected.headline: U.S. measles cases hit 35-year high in 2026
reasons: The draft states '2,318 measles cases in the first seven months of 2026' but the brief's single data_point does not provide this specific number. The data_point only claims 'More measles cases have been reported in the United States in the last two years than in all the years from 2000 through 2024 combined'—it does not confirm the 2,318 figure or the 'first seven months' timeframe. This figure appears to be smuggled from the source without appearing in the research brief.

The draft claims this marks a '35-year high' and exceeds the entire 2025 total, but neither claim appears in the brief's data_points. The brief's open_questions explicitly ask 'What are the specific 2026 case numbers and how do they compare to 2025 totals?' and 'What is the 35-year baseline and why is this year considered a 35-year high?'—indicating these facts were not confirmed in the research phase.

rejected.headline: ICC Members Vote to Remove Chief Prosecutor Khan Over Misconduct Findings
reasons: The draft states 'A panel of three judges, reviewing findings from a United Nations Office of Internal Oversight Services (OIOS) investigation conducted from November 2024 to December 2025, concluded that Khan engaged in an improper sexual relationship with a junior ICC staff member.' This misattributes the conclusion: the brief data shows the OIOS investigation identified the allegations, but the panel of three judges reviewed the OIOS findings and determined the serious misconduct—the conclusion came from the judicial panel, not from the OIOS investigation itself. The sentence structure conflates investigator findings with judicial determination.

The draft presents Khan's denial and legal challenge in the bear_case voice but does not surface it as a substantive counter-narrative in the main body until the final paragraphs. The bear_case material (Khan's denial and procedural challenges) should appear earlier or with clearer prominence to balance the investigative findings presented in the opening and middle sections.

rejected.headline: U.S. and Iran Trade Strikes; Trump Proposes Using Frozen Assets for War Damages
reasons: The draft states 'Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff Dan Caine scheduled to attend' the London conference. The brief lists this person as 'Dan Caine' but this name does not appear in any public record as the actual Chairman; this is either a misspelling (likely Cody) or a fabricated identity not verified in the source. The writer must trace this name back to the BBC article or reject it entirely.

The draft includes a detail that 'Netanyahu is scheduled to attend a service for the late Senator Lindsey Graham' in the brief's data point, but this claim is dropped entirely from the draft. The draft only states Netanyahu is 'scheduled to meet Trump at the White House on Tuesday.' This is not a factual error in the draft itself, but if the brief includes it as a reported claim, its omission may represent selective briefing; however, this is acceptable editorial judgment on relevance. The 'Dan Caine' name error stands as untraced.

rejected.headline: 11 indicted in deaths of six migrants found in Texas cargo container
reasons: Draft states 'six migrants' but the PBS NewsHour URL in sources contains '7-migrants' in the title. The brief itself flags this discrepancy in open_questions: 'The snippet mentions six migrants but the PBS headline references 7 migrants.' The draft resolves this ambiguity without sourcing the resolution, smuggling a factual choice not justified by the brief.
